description: Appointed Harvey Schwartz as CEO, replacing Kewsong Lee who departed abruptly in August 2022. Schwartz, former Goldman Sachs president and CFO, took the helm in February 2023 with a mandate to accelerate growth, reset firm culture, and restore investor confidence after a turbulent leadership transition.
stated_rationale: Bring proven Wall Street operational discipline and capital markets expertise to re-energize fundraising, strengthen the credit platform, and professionalize management beyond the founding generation.
observable_metric_impact: Under Schwartz (2023-2025): total AUM grew from ~382B to ~477B (+25%); fee-related earnings improved; announced $40B fundraising target for 2024; share price recovered from 2022 lows. Firm articulated clear medium-term FRE and DE growth targets at 2024 Investor Day.
op_prereq_summary: Required board consensus among three co-founders to approve external hire; necessitated compensation package competitive with bulge-bracket peers; demanded rapid strategic plan articulation to stabilize LP relationships following Kewsong Lee's unexpected departure.

description: Acquired majority stake in Fortitude Re, a reinsurance company with ~$50B in insurance reserves, becoming one of the first mega-alt managers (alongside Apollo/Athene) to secure a captive insurance capital channel. Carlyle progressively increased its ownership stake to approximately 71% by 2024.
stated_rationale: Secure a permanent, long-duration capital pool to anchor Carlyle's credit and investment-grade strategies, replicating the Apollo-Athene model of pairing insurance liabilities with private credit origination to generate durable fee streams and investment income.
observable_metric_impact: Fortitude Re contributed a significant portion of Carlyle's credit AUM growth, helping Global Credit reach $211B by Q4 2025. Insurance-linked assets provide sticky, long-duration fee base. Carlyle recognized balance-sheet investment income from Fortitude Re holdings. Catalyzed repositioning of Carlyle's earnings mix toward more predictable fee-related revenue.
op_prereq_summary: Required state insurance regulatory approval across multiple jurisdictions; necessitated building out dedicated insurance asset management team and ALM capabilities; demanded integration of reinsurance portfolio management with Carlyle's existing credit origination platform.

description: Expanded credit platform to $211B AUM by Q4 2025, surpassing private equity ($164B) as Carlyle's largest segment. Growth driven by CLO issuance, direct lending scale-up, and insurance-linked mandates through Fortitude Re. Launched multiple new credit products including opportunistic credit and asset-backed finance vehicles.
stated_rationale: Rebalance Carlyle's historically PE-dominant revenue mix toward higher-fee-margin, more recurring credit management fees. Credit AUM generates steadier fee-related earnings versus PE's lumpy carry realization cycle, improving earnings visibility and multiple re-rating potential.
observable_metric_impact: Credit segment grew from ~$147B (2022) to ~$211B (2025), a 44% increase over three years. Credit now represents ~44% of total AUM versus ~34% of total AUM. Carlyle became one of the top-5 CLO managers globally. Management fee rate on credit AUM stabilized in the 50-60 bps range, supporting FRE growth.
op_prereq_summary: Required hiring and integration of senior credit professionals; leveraged Fortitude Re insurance capital as anchor allocation; demanded expansion of CLO structuring and direct lending origination capabilities beyond legacy distressed/opportunistic credit roots.

description: Scaled AlpInvest Partners (acquired 2011) into a $102B investment solutions platform offering secondaries, co-investments, and primary fund-of-funds strategies. AlpInvest became Carlyle's third major segment alongside Global PE and Global Credit, providing diversified private-markets access to institutional and increasingly wealth-channel investors.
stated_rationale: Leverage AlpInvest's established multi-manager platform to capture growing LP demand for diversified private-markets access, secondaries liquidity, and co-investment programs. Position Carlyle to compete in the investment-solutions space against StepStone, Hamilton Lane, and Partners Group.
observable_metric_impact: AlpInvest AUM reached $102B by Q4 2025, up from ~$73B in 2022 (+40%). The segment contributed meaningfully to management fees with relatively capital-light operations. Secondaries and co-investment strategies attracted new LP commitments, including sovereign wealth funds and pension systems seeking portfolio construction solutions.
op_prereq_summary: Required continued investment in AlpInvest's Amsterdam-based team and deal sourcing infrastructure; demanded integration of AlpInvest distribution with Carlyle's global LP relationships; necessitated product innovation in semi-liquid and wealth-channel formats to broaden investor access beyond traditional institutional LPs.
